author: dgusakov
content: You are almost right here. But there is a catch, as I see it. Assume we take the state as you described. Since we need to account for both EL depsoits and CL withdrawals, we are in the situation where withdrawals from `block_X` on CL are reflected in EL block with `block_hash`, but depiosits from the same EL block are not in CL `block_X`, since they will be applied on CL in `block_X+1`. Correct me if I'm wrong here

author: dgusakov
content: One may think that we can just take deposits from CL `block_X+1`, but since there are also other state transitions here, we will be in the situation where we need to rebuild `pending_deposits` on the Oracle side (simulate state transition partially where we only process EL deposits and not do the full state transition). So the ultimate problem here is that with epbs + current PR there will be no such thing as CL + EL block pairs where the state is consistent. I admit that our assumption about how `alpha.4` behaves might have been wrong and the issue is less in the current PR but more in epbs in general. Hence, it will be great to hear from CL devs what the new approach is to fetch a consistent EL + CL state where there will be no withdrawn or deposited ETH missing on both sides and there will be no ETH that is reflected twice. Appreciate your help here

author: dgusakov
content: We have raised it because it will likely affect more applications. Hence, we need to have a clear understading about the solution

author: nflaig
content:
I don't think `pending_deposits` are the issue if you do the following:
- get `block_X`
- get `cl_state` (for `block_X.state_root`)
- `block_hash` is `block_X.body.signed_execution_payload_bid.message.parent_block_hash`, or `cl_state.latest_block_hash`
- fetch EL state with `block_hash`

author: nflaig
content: I do think withdrawals need to be considered though because `cl_state` has already deducted them from validator balances (via `apply_withdrawals` in `process_withdrawals`) but those are not reflected in EL state for `block_hash`, only the next EL block that builds on top of`block_hash` includes those, the `cl_state` includes those via `state.payload_expected_withdrawals`

author: dgusakov
content: Yes. This is the problem with looking at the next CL block. So the concern remains. There is not clear way to get consitent state

author: nflaig
content: so the problem really is that withdrawals are pre-deducted on the CL side

author: dgusakov
content: We can think about it this way, yes

author: nflaig
content: but you have those in the state via `cl_state.payload_expected_withdrawals` so you can add them back to validator balances

author: dgusakov
content: <@565595741477928990> This looks like a simpler option compared to deposits inclusion simulation. Tho it works if we take CL `block_X` and parent EL block for the `cl_state.latest_block_hash`. So that we have all effects from this EL block reflected and we need to add all withdrawals back to the validator balances. I general it does not look too bad to me. If <@565595741477928990> has no objections I would vote for having an explicit comment in CL spec covering the case "How to get consistent state on CL + EL"

author: nflaig
content: but let's see if <@755590043632140352> or <@592004585506340885> can confirm this, I am not that familiar why we need to pre-deduct withdrawals from CL balances but I am sure there is good reason for it, maybe there is a more elegant solution?

author: nflaig
content: but this is true in epbs in general, you need to look at the next CL block to know whether previous block was FULL or EMPTY

author: nflaig
content:
so I am not even sure why we need to pre-deduct withdrawals from CL side
- in `process_withdrawals` we can remove `apply_withdrawals(state, expected.withdrawals)`
- and then in `apply_parent_execution_payload` we can run `apply_withdrawals(state, state.payload_expected_withdrawals)`
so I believe my #5094 changes can even solve that issue if we do this

author: george_avs
content:
If withdrawals are also deferred to the next slot, that would make both directions symmetric and simplify things significantly — not just working with cross-layer state, but also the overall mental model of the system.

To echo what Dima said — the problem is not with our oracle specifically, we'll figure that out. What we wanted to flag is the asymmetry itself. There are many protocols that may do cross-layer TVL calculations, and this is not an obvious thing to catch. If someone in DeFi misses this nuance, it could lead to serious accounting issues.

If it can be resolved the way you're suggesting, that would be great. If not, I agree with Dima that it would be valuable to educate the community about the nuances of ePBS — it's a big change and things like this are easy to miss.

author: potuz
content: The withdrawals are sent to the EL on the attributes before the next block is generated. The next block will deduct them and the next Payload will credit them in the EL. The problem is that there's a state transition between the two and the balance may not be enough to deduct the withdrawal after that state transition. So we need to deduct immediately to escrow what surely needs to be minted in the EL

author: nflaig
content: good point, I don't think it's that trivial to resolve, I am exploring the spec a bit in that direction to see what could work

author: potuz
content: There can't be any solution without the intermediate state transition

author: potuz
content: I don't think this warrants a consensus change over though
